title: Hurvin Anderson's luminous palette
gist: A Tate Britain retrospective gives overdue recognition to Hurvin Anderson, a British-Jamaican painter whose luminous work - one piece sold for over GBP 7m - sits in dialogue with British landscape tradition while chronicling Windrush migration and Afro-Caribbean life.

insights.claim: Hurvin Anderson's 1998 painting of a leisure-centre swimming pool, 'Audition', sold at Christie's for over seven million pounds in 2021.
insights.so_what: Concrete auction benchmark for a living British artist's top-tier work.
insights.speaker: Duncan Wheeler

insights.claim: Anderson reached his sixties before receiving a retrospective of the stature his work deserves, which the reviewer frames as a warning against complacency about undervalued artists.
insights.so_what: Reputation-lag signal: institutional recognition trailing market and critical value.
insights.speaker: Duncan Wheeler

insights.claim: Anderson was shortlisted for the Turner Prize in 2017 and has drawn commissions from the Perez Art Museum Miami (a 16-panel 'Passenger Opportunity') and Lincoln College, Oxford (a 600th-anniversary tapestry).
insights.so_what: Track record of institutional demand supporting his rising standing.
insights.speaker: Duncan Wheeler

insights.claim: The Tate Britain retrospective (until 23 August 2026) comprises more than 80 works across six rooms, with four new pieces addressing colonialism, memory, Rastafarianism and migration.
insights.so_what: Scope and dating of the show for anyone tracking the exhibition.
insights.speaker: Duncan Wheeler

insights.claim: Anderson's luminous, vibrant palette is positioned in explicit dialogue with the British landscape tradition of Turner and Constable rather than apart from it.
insights.so_what: Frames him within, not outside, the canonical British art lineage - relevant to critical valuation.
insights.speaker: Duncan Wheeler

insights.claim: Anderson's recurring barbershop and Caribbean-parlour scenes depict rare spaces where black individuals relax and feel at home, and works like 'Hawksbill Bay' show landscapes associated with wealthy, often white, patrons.
insights.so_what: The socio-political content is integral to the work's meaning and critical reception.
insights.speaker: Duncan Wheeler
